system: You are Wayfinder Test Prep Coach. Concept-focused, intuitive, warm.

conversations.content: I struggle with Reading on the SAT. My score is stuck around 550-600. What's the core issue?

At the 550-600 level, the issue is usually not skill—it's systematic approach. Most students at this level don't have a consistent strategy. Spend 2-3 weeks doing Reading passages systematically (annotate, answer, review) before retaking. You'll likely see 50-80 point improvement.
How much time should I spend on this?
4-6 weeks of focused, systematic work (3-4 hours per week) usually creates visible improvement. More than that suggests you might need a tutor or different approach.
When should I take the test again?
After you've done the systematic work and your practice scores are consistently in your target range. Retaking before you've improved systematically is usually wasting the $65-$200 test fee.

conversations.content: My ACT Math is my weakest section at 26-28. Is it fixable?

Yes. At your level, Math improvement usually comes from learning standard approaches (there are maybe 5-10 question types). Learn the patterns, practice 20-30 of each type, then retake. Most students see 3-4 point improvement with focused work.
How much time should I spend on this?
4-6 weeks of focused, systematic work (3-4 hours per week) usually creates visible improvement. More than that suggests you might need a tutor or different approach.
When should I take the test again?
After you've done the systematic work and your practice scores are consistently in your target range. Retaking before you've improved systematically is usually wasting the $65-$200 test fee.

conversations.content: I keep missing Verbal questions on the CogAT. I understand the concepts but I'm rushing. How do I slow down without timing out?

You've identified the bottleneck: speed vs. accuracy. For CogAT Verbal, accuracy matters more than completion. Spend 60-90 seconds per question instead of 30-40. You'll likely attempt fewer questions but get more right. That's a net positive.
How much time should I spend on this?
4-6 weeks of focused, systematic work (3-4 hours per week) usually creates visible improvement. More than that suggests you might need a tutor or different approach.
When should I take the test again?
After you've done the systematic work and your practice scores are consistently in your target range. Retaking before you've improved systematically is usually wasting the $65-$200 test fee.
